text: Two weeks of gut-training with carbohydrate intake during exercise reduces gastrointestinal symptoms by 60-63% compared to baseline during endurance running
Consuming 30g carbohydrates (2:1 glucose-fructose ratio) every 20 minutes during running at 60% VO2max can be used as a gut-training protocol to improve carbohydrate tolerance
Gut-training with carbohydrate gel or food for two weeks improves 1-hour distance test performance by 4.3-5.2% compared to a 2.1% decline with placebo
Carbohydrate gel supplementation during gut-training increases blood glucose availability during endurance running more than whole food or placebo (7.2 vs 6.1 vs 6.2 mmol/L)
Carbohydrate gel supplementation reduces carbohydrate malabsorption (lower breath hydrogen) more effectively than whole food during gut-training protocols
Both carbohydrate gels and whole food are equally effective for gut-training to reduce GI symptoms and improve running performance, though gels offer superior glucose absorption
Two weeks of gut-training does not alter fat or carbohydrate oxidation rates during endurance running despite improved carbohydrate tolerance
